critique: Strong alignment with Flywheel's Swiss-design system: black/off-white palette, DM Mono for data labels, square geometry, and the warm sand accent bar at top are all on-brand. The dot-field texture adds editorial depth without violating the no-gradients rule. Main issues: (1) The 'fully in-office' bar renders at 0% width with its value label overlapping the track border, making that data point unreadable — this is a significant legibility failure for the core data story. (2) The bar value '13' for fully remote sits inside a narrow fill area and may clip; '24' is fine at full width. (3) The eyebrow 'Comparison' is generic — something like 'Stanford / 16,000 workers' would be more editorial and on-brand. (4) The subtext font size (14px) feels slightly large relative to the headline (30px), compressing the hierarchy gap. (5) The scatter-plot dot field is well-executed but the line connectors between dots add visual noise without semantic purpose — remove them per restraint principles. Fix the 0% baseline bar (use a 3–4px minimum visible fill with an outside label) and the card becomes a strong, publishable asset.
visionCritique: The card successfully borrows the brand's floating dot/bubble background pattern and clean white canvas, which creates decent aesthetic continuity. However, several issues reduce polish: (1) The bar chart uses flat black bars with no color differentiation — adding a brand-accent color (e.g., warm tan/gold for 'Hybrid' to signal the winner) would create clearer visual hierarchy and more professional feel. (2) The 'Fully in-office' bar shows only '0' with virtually no bar width, making it look like a rendering bug rather than a data point — add a minimal visible baseline or label it differently. (3) The chart is vertically centered in too much negative space; tighten the vertical padding or add axis labels/units to fill the canvas purposefully. (4) The subtitle text uses a colored highlight ('The data points to one clear winner') in blue, which doesn't appear in the brand palette — align accent color to the brand's warm beige/tan tones. (5) Typography in the headline is slightly lighter weight than the brand's bold/heavy hero font — increase font weight to better match the brand's confident, heavy typographic style. (6) The overall card feels somewhat generic/default; injecting more brand-specific typographic personality and a deliberate accent color would elevate it significantly.
